title: ITLS Exam
description: International Trauma Life Support exam questions

questions.text: In a rear-impact motor vehicle collision, which area of the spine is most susceptible to injury?
questions.explanation: The sudden increase in acceleration produces posterior displacement of the occupants and possible hyperextension of the cervical spine if the headrest is not properly adjusted. The potential for cervical spine injuries is great.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 36-year-old male sustains blunt force thoracic trauma and fits the criteria for a load-and-go patient. Which of the following should be performed on the scene?
options: Obtain a finger-stick serum lactate level
Assess for other potentially life-threatening conditions
questions.explanation: The purpose of the initial assessment is to prioritize the patient and to identify all immediately life-threatening conditions. The information gathered is used to make decisions about critical interventions and time of transport.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The use of external laryngeal manipulation:
options: Decreases the risk of airway trauma
questions.explanation: Manipulating the thyroid cartilage can help bring the vocal cords into view during endotracheal intubation. This is called external laryngeal manipulation (ELM). The movement is usually pressing the thyroid cartilage backward against the esophagus and then upward and slightly to the patient’s right side.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A pericardiocentesis is performed to:
options: To remove fluid from the lining around the lungs
To remove fluid from the lining around the heart
To inject medications directly into the heart
questions.explanation: The pericardial sac is an inelastic membrane that surrounds the heart. If blood collects rapidly between the heart and pericardium from a cardiac injury, the ventricles of the heart will be compressed, making the heart less able to refill, and cardiac output falls.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 56-year-old male sustains a gunshot wound to the abdomen. Vital signs are BP 74/32, P 136 present only at the carotid, and R 24 and shallow. The target of fluid resuscitation is:
questions.explanation: This is an example of internal, uncontrolled hemorrhage. Administer sufficient normal saline to maintain peripheral perfusion, following local or EMS agency medical direction policies. Maintaining peripheral perfusion is generally defined as giving enough fluid—usually in boluses—to return a peripheral pulse, such as a radial pulse.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An unconscious 15-year-old male was struck on the head with a baseball bat. His arms and legs are extended and his right pupil is dilated and fixed. You should:
options: Insert an oropharyngeal airway, administer oxygen, and restrict fluids
Apply a non-rebreather mask, restrict fluids, and administer Mannitol
Perform nasotracheal intubation, restrict fluids, and provide adequate ventilation
Perform bag-mask ventilation at 20 per minute
questions.explanation: The classic findings of this life-threatening situation are a decreasing level of consciousness (LOC) that rapidly progresses to coma, dilation of the pupil and an outward—downward deviation of the eye on the side of the injury, paralysis of the arm and leg on the side opposite the injury, or decerebrate posturing (arms and legs extended). The danger of immediate herniation outweighs the risk of cerebral ischemia that can follow hyperventilation. The cerebral herniation syndrome is the only situation in which hyperventilation is still indicated. (You must ventilate every three seconds [20/minute] for adults.)
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following mechanisms of injury does not commonly cause damage to the spinal cord?
questions.explanation: Certain mechanisms of trauma can overcome the protective properties, injuring the spinal column and cord. The most common mechanisms are hyperextension, hyperflexion, compression, and rotation. Less commonly, lateral stress or distraction will injure the cord.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 4-year-old female is unconscious after falling off a bicycle. Which of the following is the best method to open the airway?
questions.explanation: Look for signs of airway obstruction in the child, including apnea, stridor, and “gurgling” respirations. If identified, perform a jaw-thrust maneuver without moving the neck.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: When performing the ITLS Primary Survey, the team leader may minimize errors by:
options: Permitting crew to continue the assessment
questions.explanation: This team approach makes the most efficient use of time and allows you to rapidly perform the initial assessment without becoming distracted by performing the necessary interventions yourself, which can interrupt your thought process and cause errors.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: You have placed an NPA in your patient and now observe mild hemorrhage from the nares. You should:
options: Immediately remove the NPA and pack the nose with gauze
Immediately remove the NPA and reinsert on the opposite nare
Leave the NPA in place so as not to disturb the clot or reactivate bleeding
Contact medical control as an NPA will not cause hemorrhage
questions.explanation: Mild hemorrhage from the nose after insertion of the airway is not an indication to remove it. In fact, it is probably better to keep an NPA in place so as not to disturb the clot or reactivate the bleeding.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 27-year-old male with blunt chest trauma from a motor vehicle collision was successfully intubated at the scene. While ventilating the patient, you note resistance with absence of right chest wall movement. You should suspect a:
questions.explanation: The development of decreased lung compliance (difficulty in squeezing the bag-mask device) in the intubated patient should always alert you to the possibility of a tension pneumothorax.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 34-year-old man has a gunshot wound to the right groin area. Arterial bleeding, which cannot be controlled with direct pressure or tourniquet, is coming from the wound. The patient appears confused, diaphoretic, and has weak peripheral pulses. What is the appropriate fluid resuscitation regimen for this patient?
options: Intravenous fluid at a "keep open" rate
Intravenous fluid; give enough fluid to maintain peripheral pulses
Intravenous fluid at a "wide open" rate
No intravenous access should be established in this situation
questions.explanation: Give only enough normal saline to maintain a blood pressure high enough for adequate peripheral perfusion. Maintaining peripheral perfusion may be defined as producing a peripheral pulse (such as a radial pulse).
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The gold standard to confirm that the endotracheal tube is placed in the trachea is:
options: Presence of condensation in the tube
questions.explanation: Although the most reliable method of ensuring proper placement is actually visualizing the tube passing through the glottic opening, even this is not 100% certain. In fact, it is only reliable for the moment you see it. The gold standard for confirming and monitoring ETT placement is waveform capnography.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the causes of pediatric injury listed below is most likely to result in severe injury or death?
questions.explanation: Injuries sustained by children riding bicycles, motorcycles, and dirt bikes, especially if they are not wearing a helmet, are often severe. Motor-vehicle collisions — especially if car seats, booster seats, and lap-belt restraints are improperly used — may result in seat-belt syndrome, which can include life-threatening injuries to the liver, spleen, intestines and/or lumbar spine.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following injuries would change a trauma patient's transport classification from 'stable' to 'load and go'?
questions.explanation: Conditions that can rapidly lead to shock include penetrating wounds to the torso, abnormal chest exam, tender distended abdomen, pelvic instability and bilateral femur fractures.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following findings would not make a patient difficult to ventilate with a bag-valve mask?
questions.explanation: Predictors of difficult mask ventilation can be remembered using the “BOOTS” mnemonic: B- Beards, O - Obesity, O - Older patients, T- Toothlessness, S- Snores or stridor
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What is the most frequent cause for an intubated trauma patient to develop poor lung compliance while being ventilated?
questions.explanation: The development of decreased lung compliance (difficulty in squeezing the bag-mask device) in the intubated patient should always alert you to the possibility of a tension pneumothorax.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Hemostatic agents applied directly to the source of bleeding must be used in conjunction with:
options: Pressure points to arteries proximal to the wound
Elevation of the wound above the level of the heart
questions.explanation: Pack the hemostatic agent in the wound and hold firm pressure. The hemostatic agent is an “adjunct” to assist in controlling hemorrhage, not a hemorrhage control by itself.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Routine use of hyperventilation in the traumatic brain injury (TBI) patient will:
options: Cause vasoconstriction and increased cerebral ischemia
Cause vasodilation and decreased intracranial pressure (ICP)
Cause an increase of end-tidal CO2
questions.explanation: Hyperventilation actually has only a slight effect on brain swelling, but causes a significant decrease in cerebral perfusion from that same vasoconstriction, resulting in cerebral hypoxia. Thus, both hyperventilation and hypoventilation can cause cerebral ischemia and increased mortality in the TBI patient.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which finding requires interruption of the ITLS Primary Survey?
questions.explanation: You may interrupt the assessment sequence only if (1) the scene becomes unsafe, (2) you must treat exsanguinating hemorrhage, (3) you must treat an airway obstruction, or (4) you must treat cardiac arrest. (Respiratory arrest, dyspnea, or bleeding management should be delegated to other team members while you continue assessment of the patient.)
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Bag-valve-mask ventilation:
options: Rarely causes gastric distention due to low airway pressures
Is more effective in patients whose dentures have been removed
Is easily accomplished by one rescuer at the head
May exceed 60 cm H20 pressure in the airway
questions.explanation: Bag-valve-mask ventilation has challenges because pressures generated by squeezing the bag may equal or exceed 60 cm H20.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is true regarding pulmonary contusion?
options: It is uncommon in chest trauma
It is caused by hemorrhage into the lung parenchyma
It is only caused by blunt force trauma
questions.explanation: Pulmonary contusion is a very common chest injury. It is caused by hemorrhage into lung parenchyma secondary to blunt force trauma or penetrating injury such as a missile. It occurs commonly with flail segment or multiple rib fractures. A pulmonary contusion takes hours to develop and rarely develops during prehospital care.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 25-year-old female was stabbed in the left arm. She presents with an altered mental status and signs of shock. Bleeding is controlled by direct pressure and use of a tourniquet. Which intravenous fluid therapy is appropriate?
options: Estimate blood loss and administer fluid at a 3:1 ratio
Fluid administration to maintain systolic pressure of 60 mmHg
Intravenous fluid administration bolus of 500 mL, then reassess
questions.explanation: When bleeding is controlled, give normal saline as a bolus (500 mL in adults; 20 mL/kg in pediatric patients) rapidly and then repeat the ITLS Reassessment Exam.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: In the absence of herniation syndrome, adult patients with suspected traumatic brain injury should be ventilated as a rate of:
questions.explanation: Hyperventilation and hypoventilation can cause cerebral ischemia and increased mortality in the TBI patient. Maintain normal ventilation (not hyperventilation) with high-flow oxygen at a rate of about one breath every 6 to 8 seconds (8 to 10 per minute) to maintain an end-tidal CO2 (ETCO2) of 35-45 mmHg.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What is the most likely cause of unequal pupils in a patient whose mental status is altered due to a head injury?
questions.explanation: Suspect head injury unless patient is alert, then suspect eye injury.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 57-year-old male has a respiratory rate of 36, ETCO2 of 30 mmHg, and an oxygen saturation of 80%. You should:
options: Coach the patient to slow his breathing as his ETCO2 level indicates hyperventilation
Ventilate with a BVM at a higher rate to increase the patient's oxygen levels
Ventilate with a BVM at a rate of 6 to increase the patient's ETCO2 level
questions.explanation: Supplemental oxygen is recommended for all trauma patients, especially if hypoxic.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 31-year-old male presents with difficulty breathing, rapid and weak pulse, flat neck veins, and midline trachea. Your assessment of the left side of the chest reveals decreased breath sounds and dullness to percussion. You should suspect:
questions.explanation: Clinical signs of shock may be apparent. The neck veins are usually flat secondary to profound hypovolemia, but may very rarely be distended due to mediastinal compression. Other signs of hemothorax include decreased breath sounds and dullness to percussion on the affected side.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 45-year-old female is found unconscious at the scene of a motor vehicle collision. Her vital signs are BP 80/40, P 130, and R 30. Which of the following is the most likely cause for her vital signs?
options: Bleeding into the chest or abdomen
Spinal cord injury with neurogenic shock
questions.explanation: Hypovolemic shock victims usually have tachycardia, are pale, and have flat neck veins. So, if you find a trauma victim with a fast heart rate, who is pale, with weak radial pulses and flat neck veins, this patient is probably bleeding from some injury, either internally or externally (or possibly both).
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The cause of a secondary brain injury is:
questions.explanation: Secondary brain injury is the result of hypoxia and/or decreased perfusion of brain tissue.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which one of the following is a reason to interrupt the initial assessment?
options: Severe head injury with brain tissue visible
questions.explanation: Remember, once you begin patient assessment in the ITLS Primary Survey, only four things should cause you to interrupt completion of the assessment. You may interrupt the assessment sequence only if (1) the scene becomes unsafe, (2) you must treat exsanguinating hemorrhage, (3) you must treat an airway obstruction, or (4) you must treat cardiac arrest.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is not a desired characteristic of a suction device?
options: It can be carried in an airway kit with an oxygen cylinder and other airway equipment
It can be hand powered or battery powered
It can generate sufficient suction and volume displacement to remove pieces of food, blood clots, and thick secretions from the oropharynx
It can be powered by your portable O2 cylinder so it is not dependent on battery power
questions.explanation: It can be hand-powered or battery-powered rather than oxygen-driven.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What is the most serious early complication of burns from electrical contact?
questions.explanation: The most serious and immediate injury that results from electrical contact is cardiac dysrhythmia.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following changes is most useful to monitor in the child with head injury?
questions.explanation: Changing level of consciousness is the best indicator of traumatic brain injury.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A disoriented 23-year-old male is injured in a motorcycle collision. The patient appears to be intoxicated and does not want medical attention despite a large laceration on his scalp, which is actively bleeding. You should:
options: Have the patient call a friend or family member and have them drive him home and sleep it off
Discuss the impact of alcohol abuse with him
Treat him as a head injured patient
Wait until the patient loses consciousness and then transport
questions.explanation: An altered mental status can be seen in every form of substance abuse. However, remember that an altered level of consciousness is always due to a head injury, shock, or hypoglycemia until proven otherwise. Also remember that all patients have an emergency medical condition until proven otherwise.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: When performing a needle decompression, which of the following is the best anatomical location?
options: Directly over the top of the fifth rib in the anterior axillary line
Directly over the top of the fourth rib in anterior axillary line
Directly under the bottom of the third rib in the midclavicular line
Directly over the top of the second rib in the anterior axillary line
questions.explanation: Insert the needle into the intercostal space at a 90-degree angle to the superior border of the fifth rib to avoid the neurovascular bundle.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is true regarding trauma in the elderly?
options: Elderly patients are less likely to bleed internally than younger patients
Elderly patients have better outcomes following burns than younger patients
Fatal outcomes are more likely in the elderly than in the young
Motor vehicle collisions are an uncommon mechanism of injury
questions.explanation: Geriatric patients can die from less severe injuries than younger patients. In addition, it is often difficult to separate the effects of the aging process or of a chronic illness from the consequences of an injury.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following organs will suffer the least structural damage from a gunshot wound from a rifle?
questions.explanation: Generally, damage done is proportional to tissue density. Highly dense organs such as bone, muscle, and the liver sustain more damage than less dense organs such as the lungs.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following should always be performed prior to transport?
questions.explanation: The following procedures are done at the scene: control major external bleeding, open and maintain a patent airway (position, sweep, suction; intubate if indicated and necessary), ventilate, apply oxygen, initiate CPR, seal sucking chest wounds, stabilize flail segments, decompress tension pneumothorax when indicated, stabilize penetrating objects, and maintain SMR if indicated.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following distinguishes a tension pneumothorax from a simple pneumothorax?
questions.explanation: Clinical signs of a tension pneumothorax include dyspnea, anxiety, tachypnea, distended neck veins, and possibly tracheal deviation away from the affected side. Auscultation will reveal diminished breath sounds on the affected side and will be accompanied by hyperresonance when percussed. Shock with hypotension will follow and is not present with a simple pneumothorax.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 25-year-old female was stabbed in the left arm. She presents with an altered mental status and signs of shock. Bleeding is uncontrolled despite direct pressure. You should:
options: Apply a tourniquet directly over the wound
Elevate the extremity above the level of the heart
Apply a tourniquet proximal to the injury
questions.explanation: For uncontrolled hemorrhage, do not hesitate to apply a tourniquet to a bleeding extremity to stop severe bleeding that cannot be otherwise controlled. If you cannot stop severe bleeding with pressure and cannot use a tourniquet (groin, axilla, neck, face, scalp), you may use one of the hemostatic agents. The hemostatic agent is an “adjunct” to assist in controlling hemorrhage, not a hemorrhage control by itself.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An unresponsive 36-year-old female was involved in a motorcycle collision. Vital signs are BP 170/100, P 50, R 8 and GCS 3. You should suspect:
questions.explanation: When the intracranial pressure increases, the systemic blood pressure increases to try to preserve blood flow to the brain. The body senses the rise in systemic blood pressure, and this triggers a drop in the pulse rate as the body tries to lower the systemic blood pressure.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 42-year-old male is involved in a motorcycle collision. Which of the following assessment findings should be managed first?
questions.explanation: If your patient has major bleeding, the priority is C-A-B-C. The first C stands for control life-threatening bleeding. (Do not confuse this with the American Heart Association/ILCOR’s “CAB” for cardiac arrest, where the C stands for compressions.) If your patient has major external bleeding, you must immediately control it.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 32-year-old male is involved in a motor vehicle collision. The steering wheel is bent. Your assessment reveals present and equal bilateral breath sounds and a rapid and weak radial pulse that disappears upon inspiration. You should suspect:
questions.explanation: Pulsus paradoxus, or paradoxical pulse, may be noted. This is where the radial pulse is not felt with inspiration. The major differential diagnosis in the field is tension pneumothorax. With cardiac tamponade, the patient will be in shock with equal breath sounds and a midline.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Medical Director requests application of a second tourniquet. Which of the following is the appropriate site?
questions.explanation: It is very rare for a tourniquet not to control severe extremity bleeding. Consider application of a second tourniquet in this situation. A second tourniquet should be applied just below the first one. Do not take the first tourniquet down to reapply it.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 35-year-old male is alert and oriented at the scene of a motor vehicle collision complaining of knee pain and a headache. Your assessment reveals a contusion above the left eye, swelling to the left knee and unequal pupils (left is dilated). Vital signs are: BP 116/72, P 88 and R 16. You should suspect:
questions.explanation: If the patient has a normal level of consciousness, the dilated pupil is not from head injury (more likely due to eye trauma or drugs such as atropine).
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: You have a patient with an isolated stab wound to the lateral chest. According to recent studies, which of the following procedures should be avoided?
questions.explanation: Immobilization onto a long backboard is not indicated in penetrating wounds of torso, neck, or head unless there is clinical evidence of a spine injury.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What is the most common cause of cardiopulmonary arrest in the trauma patient?
questions.explanation: Hypoxemia is the most common cause of traumatic cardiopulmonary arrest. Acute airway obstruction or ineffective breathing will be clinically manifested as hypoxemia.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 34-year-old female was struck by a vehicle, causing blunt force chest trauma. She is unconscious with gasping respirations, almost no air movement, a rapid, thready carotid pulse and flat neck veins. You should:
questions.explanation: Primary goals in treating the patient with chest trauma are the following: Ensure an open airway while protecting the cervical spine; Administer high-flow oxygen and ventilate if necessary; Stabilize flail segments; Seal sucking chest wounds; Decompress the chest if needed; Load and go to appropriate level of care; Obtain venous access; Transport to appropriate level of care; Notify medical direction
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 34-year-old male is injured in a motor vehicle collision. He complains of diffuse abdominal tenderness. Vital signs are BP 100/60, P 120 and R 28 and shallow. You should:
options: Administer sufficient intravenous fluids to maintain peripheral pulses
Complete spinal motion restriction, start ventilations as needed, and transport immediately
Administer intravenous fluids to maintain a 130 mmHg systolic BP
questions.explanation: This patient is in compensated shock from possible internal hemorrhage. Administer sufficient normal saline to maintain peripheral perfusion, following local or EMS agency medical direction policies. Maintaining peripheral perfusion is generally defined as giving enough fluid—usually in boluses—to return a peripheral pulse, such as a radial pulse.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An unresponsive 52-year-old male was struck in the head by the bucket of a crane. Vital signs are BP 134/80, P 88 and R 8 and shallow. The patient should be ventilated at a rate of:
questions.explanation: Hyperventilation and hypoventilation can cause cerebral ischemia and increased mortality in the TBI patient. Maintaining normal ventilation (not hyperventilation) with high-flow oxygen at a rate of about one breath every 6 to 8 seconds (8 to 10 per minute) to maintain an end-tidal CO2 (ETCO2) of 35-45.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Upon arrival at a trauma scene, you should complete a(n):
questions.explanation: It is a critical part of trauma assessment and begins before you approach the patient. If you fail to perform the preliminary steps of scene size-up, you may jeopardize your life as well as the life of your patient.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Prolonged scene times may reflect:
questions.explanation: When immediate interventions are needed, delegate them to your team members while you continue the assessment. This is an important concept that immediately addresses problems encountered and yet does not interrupt the assessment sequence and does not increase scene time. Teamwork is essential to good patient outcomes.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following indicates a state of hyperventilation?
options: An adult respiratory rate greater than 20 per minute
A tidal volume of less than 400 ml
An end tidal carbon dioxide level less than 30 mmHg
questions.explanation: A carbon dioxide level below 35 mmHg indicates hyperventilation.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An unresponsive 34-year-old female was struck by a motor vehicle. You observe asymmetrical chest wall movement with a flail segment on the right. Following delegation of c-spine control and opening the airway, you should:
questions.explanation: A large flail segment can be stabilized with a bulky dressing to decrease pain associated with rib motion during respirations. Although oxygen administration is indicated it should be administered at 12-15 lpm.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 23-year-old female fell from a second-floor balcony. Upon arrival, you find her lying in the grass. She responds to verbal commands and your assessment reveals flat neck veins, and normal chest, abdomen and pelvis examinations. Vital signs are BP 74/40, P 54 and weak, and R 16. You should suspect:
questions.explanation: The condition causing the vascular space to be too large for a normal amount of blood has been called 'shock,' or relative hypovolemia. Neurogenic shock, commonly called spinal shock, is a form of relative hypovolemia. The clinical presentation of neurogenic shock differs from hemorrhagic shock in that there is no catecholamine release and thus no pallor (vasoconstriction), tachycardia, or sweating. The patient will have a decreased blood pressure, but the heart rate will be normal or slow, and the skin is usually warm, dry, and pink.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following regarding patient positioning for external jugular cannulation is incorrect?
options: The patient must be in the supine position
Elevate the head to distend the vein and prevent air embolism
If no suspicion of cervical-spine injury exists, turn the patient's head to the opposite side
If you suspect cervical-spine injury, the head must not be turned during cannulation
questions.explanation: The patient must be in the supine position, preferably head down, to distend the vein and to prevent air embolism.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following sets of vital signs is most compatible with a diagnosis of isolated traumatic brain injury with increasing intracranial pressure?
questions.explanation: When the ICP increases, the systemic blood pressure increases to try to preserve blood flow to the brain. The body senses the rise in systemic blood pressure, and this triggers a drop in the pulse rate as the body tries to lower the systemic blood pressure.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following statements is incorrect regarding spinal motion restriction?
options: Patients should be removed from the long spine board when it is safe and practical to do so
Neck traction should be applied to extend the neck upward during cervical collar application
Remaining on the board for prolonged periods can produce discomfort, pressure sores, and respiratory compromise
A long backboard is not indicated in penetrating wounds of torso, neck, or head unless there is clinical evidence of a spine injury
questions.explanation: 'Pulling traction' is not a prehospital option, and the term traction is not an appropriate description for motion restriction of the spine. Traction usually results in further instability of any spinal-column injury.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 15-year-old male was stabbed and has an abdominal evisceration. The patient is alert and oriented, BP 112/68, P 94 and R 18. You should:
options: Irrigate any protruding organs or viscera and gently push back into the wound
Pack the wound with a hemostatic agent and perform complete spinal motion restriction
Cover any protruding organs or viscera with gauze moistened with a hypertonic dextrose solution
Prepare the patient for transport and establish vascular access during transport
questions.explanation: The patient should be readied for immediate transport with appropriate spinal motion restriction, if indicated. Of note, penetrating trauma to the abdomen or chest with no signs of neurologic deficit does not require immobilization of the spine. Time to definitive care at the trauma center is critical. Unnecessary intervention on scene should be avoided. Once en route to an appropriate trauma center, establish two large-bore IV lines of normal saline.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 33-year-old female's leg was trapped in a piece of industrial equipment and has been extricated. She is now complaining of severe lower leg pain. Patient is alert and oriented with no signs of external hemorrhage. Vital signs are BP 132/78, P 96 and R 20. Which of the following is indicated?
options: Apply a tourniquet proximal to the injury site
questions.explanation: Proper management of fractures and dislocations will decrease the incidence of pain, disability, and serious complications. Treatment in the prehospital setting is directed at proper immobilization of the injured part by the use of an appropriate splint and padding. Pain control with appropriate analgesics is another major component of the care of injured extremities.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Establishing vascular access on scene is indicated:
options: To initiate fluid resuscitation based on the Parkland Formula
questions.explanation: Intravenous (IV) line insertion is rarely needed on scene during initial care unless delay in transport to a hospital is unavoidable. It takes hours for burn shock to develop. Therefore, the only reason to initiate IV therapy on scene is if other factors indicate a need for fluid volume or medication administration.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: In the geriatric patient, which of the following findings is most likely caused by an acute injury?
questions.explanation: Care providers must distinguish between signs and symptoms of a chronic disease and an acute problem. For instance: A geriatric patient may have nonpathologic rales, or the loss of skin elasticity and the presence of mouth breathing may not necessarily represent dehydration or dependent edema.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 31-year-old female was the restrained driver of a vehicle that hit a utility pole at moderate speed. The driver's side airbag deployed. She is alert, oriented and anxious. She reports that she is 8 months pregnant and complains of abdominal, chest and lower extremity pain. Her vital signs are BP 100/60, P 90 and R 20. Your impression and interventions are:
options: Early signs of shock treated with oxygen, fluid administration at 'keep open' rate
Anxiety treated with emotional support and transport
Normal vital signs, no intervention required except transport
Compensated shock treated with oxygen, fluid administration at 20 ml/kg
questions.explanation: Do not mistake normal vital signs in pregnant patients as signs of shock. The pregnant patient has a normal resting pulse that is 10 to 15 beats faster than usual, and the blood pressure is 10 to 15 mmHg lower than usual. However, it is also important to realize that a blood loss of 30% to 35% can occur in these patients before there is a significant change in blood pressure.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: You may have been exposed to a contaminant (air or fluid borne). You should:
options: Seek treatment within hours of the exposure
Report the exposure if testing shows a positive result
Only be concerned if the exposure was to Hepatitis B
Only be concerned if the exposure was to HIV
questions.explanation: Write a report of the incident as soon as possible. Blood tests (if any) to be done on the exposed emergency care provider depend on reports of testing of the source patient.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 24-year-old male is involved in a head-on collision. If you suspect:
options: Lower leg injuries, administer fluid resuscitation at 20 ml/kg
A head injury, administer an anti-seizure medication
A chest injury, obtain an EKG
questions.explanation: Consider a bruised chest wall as a myocardial contusion that requires monitoring of cardiac rhythm and, if available, a 12-lead ECG.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A brief neurologic exam of an altered mental status patient includes:
options: Glasgow Coma Scale, glucose check, pupil examination
Glucose check, pulse oximetry, pupil examination
Glasgow Coma Scale, corneal reflex, ETCO2
questions.explanation: If the patient has an altered mental status, do a brief neurologic exam to identify possible increased intracranial pressure (ICP). This exam should include the pupils, Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score. All patients with altered mental status should have a finger-stick glucose test performed.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following findings would indicate the airway needs to be suctioned?
options: Presence of dried blood in the nares
questions.explanation: Always immediately clear blood and secretions. You also must be alert for sounds that indicate trouble. Remember: Noisy breathing is obstructed breathing.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Overinflation of an LMA mask can cause all of the following except:
questions.explanation: Overinflation may cause malposition, loss of seal, or trauma.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 54-year-old male is involved in a motor vehicle collision. The steering wheel is bent. During your initial assessment, you note his skin is pale, radial pulses are present and breath sounds are clear. Which one of the following is most consistent with these findings?
questions.explanation: Bruising of the heart is basically the same injury as an acute myocardial infarction.
questions.hint: Bent wheel + stable lungs → cardiac contusion
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is not a potential complication of performing chest decompression?
questions.explanation: Many prefer the anterior site because, in the supine patient, air in the pleural space tends to accumulate anteriorly. Thus, there is a better chance of having the air in the pleural space removed when the anterior approach to decompressing is used. It is also easier to access when the patient is on the ambulance stretcher.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is considered one of the four essential components to maintain normal perfusion?
questions.explanation: It is important to remember that blood pressure requires a 'steady state' activity of all the preceding factors. The heart must be pumping, the blood volume must be adequate, the blood vessels must be intact, and the lungs must be oxygenating the blood.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What site is generally the easiest to landmark for intraosseous infusion?
questions.explanation: Proximal tibia, approximately 2 cm below the patella and approximately 2 cm (depending on the patient's anatomy) medial to the tibial tuberosity, or one finger breadth medial to the tibial tuberosity. This is often the easiest site to locate.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: As intracerebral pressure rises after an isolated head injury, what does the systolic blood pressure do?
questions.explanation: When the ICP increases, the systemic blood pressure increases to try to preserve blood flow to the brain. The body senses the rise in systemic blood pressure, and this triggers a drop in the pulse rate as the body tries to lower the systemic blood pressure.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which mechanism of injury meets the exclusion criteria for spinal motion restriction?
options: 14-year-old with an isolated stab wound to the midaxillary chest wall
24-year-old with a gunshot wound to the neck
15-year-old female with extremity numbness and tingling after falling backward
questions.explanation: Immobilization onto a long backboard is not indicated in penetrating wounds of torso, neck, or head unless there is clinical evidence of a spine injury.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following organs is contained in the retroperitoneal region of the abdomen?
questions.explanation: The retroperitoneal abdomen is the part of the abdomen behind the thoracic and true portions of the abdomen, separated from the other abdominal regions by a thin retroperitoneal membrane; includes the kidneys, ureters, pancreas, posterior duodenum, ascending and descending colon, abdominal aorta, and the inferior vena cava.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which treatment is indicated for the patient who is entrapped for an extended period of time with an isolated crush injury to the lower extremity?
questions.explanation: Initial administration of sodium bicarbonate should be at 1 mEq/kg bolus followed by an infusion of 0.25 mEq/kg body weight per hour of the solution.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 16-year-old female is rescued from a burning house. She has 25% partial thickness burns, and the burned areas are hot to the touch. What is the appropriate treatment?
options: Apply ice to burned areas until cool to the touch
Apply clean water to burned areas for up to 5-10 minutes
Apply iced water to burned areas until cool to the touch
Do not apply anything to burned areas other than clean sheets
questions.explanation: Limit burn wound progression as much as possible. Rapid cooling early in the course of a surface burn injury can help limit this progression. Following removal from the source of the burn, the skin and clothing are still hot, and this heat continues to injure the tissues, causing an increase in burn depth and seriousness of the injury. Cooling halts this process and, if done appropriately, is beneficial. Cooling should be done with tap water or any source of clean room-temperature water, but it should be undertaken for no more than 5 to 10 minutes. Cooling for longer periods of time can induce hypothermia and subsequent shock. Do not use ice or ice water because this may induce hypothermia.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An unresponsive 5-year-old female was struck by a car. She presents with retractions and nasal flaring. The airway is patent, breathing is fast and shallow, and the carotid pulse is weak and slow. Which of the steps in her management should be done first?
questions.explanation: Note whether the child is 'working' to breathe, demonstrated by subcostal or suprasternal retractions, nasal flaring, or grunting. If you have any doubt that the child is breathing adequately on his or her own, immediately assist the child's breathing.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following regarding trauma in the elderly is true?
options: Elderly patients are less likely to bleed internally than younger patients
Elderly patients have a better outcome following burns than younger patients
Fatal outcomes are more likely in the elderly than in the young
Motor vehicle collisions are an uncommon mechanism of injury
questions.explanation: Geriatric patients can die from less severe injuries than younger patients. In addition, it is often difficult to separate the effects of the aging process or of a chronic illness from the consequences of an injury.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Supine hypotension syndrome in the pregnant patient is caused by:
options: Uterine obstruction of venous blood flow
Uterine pressure on the vagal nerve
questions.explanation: In supine hypotension syndrome, the hypotension is caused by the weight of the pregnant uterus pressing on the inferior vena cava and decreasing the return of blood to the heart by up to 30%.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following has a greater chance of surviving traumatic cardiopulmonary arrest?
options: Patients who suffer blunt force trauma to the torso
Patients with penetrating chest trauma who are hypothermic
questions.explanation: Trauma patients who presented unconscious, without palpable pulse or spontaneous respiration, found that patients with sinus rhythm and nondilated (< 4 mm) reactive pupils had a good chance of survival.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following concerning blast injury is true?
options: Primary blast injury is caused by heat
Secondary blast injury is caused by objects propelled by the explosion
Tertiary blast injury is caused by toxic fumes
Quaternary blast injury is caused by the displacement of the body
questions.explanation: The secondary blast injury is the result of the patient being struck by material (shrapnel) propelled by the blast force.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The purpose of the ITLS Primary Survey is to:
options: Identify all immediate life threats within the first 2 minutes
Determine if the patient has any medical allergies
questions.explanation: The purpose of the initial assessment is to prioritize the patient and to identify immediately life-threatening conditions and take less than 2 minutes.
questions.hint: Primary survey → find life threats fast
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following would be the most compelling reason to immediately intubate a patient?
questions.explanation: Continued problems with mask ventilation should prompt the consideration of airway obstruction as a potential cause. Ultimately, the placement of a 'rescue' ventilation device (BIAD, such as laryngeal mask airway) or endotracheal tube may be required.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The depth of the ET tube at the teeth should be approximately ___ times the diameter of the tube.
questions.explanation: The mark on the tube that is even with the teeth should be three times the diameter of the ET tube. Thus, an 8.0 mm tube in an adult should be at 24 cm.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: During transport, a trauma patient develops shallow and rapid breathing, cyanosis and a weak, rapid carotid pulse with distended neck veins and diminished breath sounds on the right. You should:
questions.explanation: Decompress the affected side of the chest, if indicated. The indication for performing emergency chest decompression is the presence of a tension pneumothorax with decompensation as evidenced by more than one of the following: respiratory distress and cyanosis, loss of the radial pulse (late shock), decreasing level of consciousness.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 23-year-old female fell from a second-floor balcony. Upon arrival, you find her lying in the grass. She responds to verbal commands and your assessment reveals flat neck veins, and normal chest, abdomen and pelvis examinations. Her skin is cool, clammy and ashen; respirations are rapid and shallow; radial pulses are too rapid to count and thready. You place her on the heart monitor and it shows a wide-complex tachycardia of about 280 per minute. You should suspect:
questions.explanation: If cardiac output falls (either due to dropping or a very fast heart rate or lowered stroke volume), blood pressure will fall.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is the correct orientation for inserting an intraosseous needle to the proximal tibia?
options: Medial to the midline, avoiding the growth plate
Medial to the midline, pointing toward the growth plate
Lateral to the midline, avoiding the growth plate
Lateral to the midline, pointing toward the growth plate
questions.explanation: Approximately 2 cm below the patella and approximately 2 cm (depending on the patient's anatomy) medial to the tibial tuberosity, or one finger breadth medial to the tibial tuberosity. This is often the easiest site to locate.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Barbiturates should not be administered as a sedative for the head-injured patient because they:
questions.explanation: Careful use of benzodiazepines can decrease agitation without dropping blood pressure. An added benefit to the use of benzodiazepines is that they prevent seizures. Seizure prophylaxis in the head injured patient should be initiated on the recommendation of medical direction. Other agents suitable for use include phenytoin. Do not use barbiturates because they can cause hypotension.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: In which situation should an Emergency Rescue be performed?
options: Leaking antifreeze from a vehicle radiator
questions.explanation: Emergency Rescue is reserved for those situations in which there is immediate (within seconds) environmental threat to the life of the victim and/or emergency care responder. Patients should be moved to a safe area in a manner that places the emergency care responder at the least risk. Rapid extrication should be considered for patients whose medical conditions or situations require fast intervention (1 or 2 minutes— but not seconds) to prevent death.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is the most common cause of abdominal trauma?
questions.explanation: Blunt trauma is the most common mechanism of abdominal injury and has relatively high mortality rates of 10% to 30%. The reason is likely related to the frequency of accompanying injuries to the head, chest, pelvis, and/or an extremity in as many as 70% of motor vehicle collision victims.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Proper immobilization of a forearm (radius and ulna) fracture includes splinting the:
questions.explanation: Use a splint that will immobilize one joint above and one joint below the injury site.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 16-year-old female receives 25% partial thickness thermal burns. You should:
options: Clean and cover the burns with an antimicrobial sheet
Clean and apply ice packs to the burned area
Cool the burn and then protect from heat loss
questions.explanation: Limit burn wound progression as much as possible. Rapid cooling early in the course of a surface burn injury can help limit this progression. Following removal from the source of the burn, the skin and clothing are still hot, and this heat continues to injure the tissues, causing an increase in burn depth and seriousness of the injury. Cooling halts this process and, if done appropriately, is beneficial. Cooling should be done with tap water or any source of clean room-temperature water, but it should be undertaken for no more than 5 to 10 minutes. Cooling for longer periods of time can induce hypothermia and subsequent shock. Do not use ice or ice water because this may induce hypothermia.
questions.hint: Burns → cool then prevent heat loss
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 3-year-old male fell 9 feet (3 meters). He has a decreased level of consciousness and is making persistent 'grunting' sounds with respirations. Your initial treatment is to:
options: Apply a cervical collar and transport immediately
Suction the patient and apply oxygen with a nasal cannula
Provide ventilatory support with supplementary oxygen
Quickly complete a rapid trauma survey
questions.explanation: Note whether the child is 'working' to breathe, demonstrated by subcostal or suprasternal retractions, nasal flaring, or grunting. Look at the chest rise, listen for air going in and out, and feel the air coming out of the nose. If there is no movement, reposition the jaw to remove any anatomical obstruction. If you still do not sense any air exchange, you must breathe for the child. If you have any doubt that the child is breathing adequately on his or her own, immediately assist the child's breathing.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 78-year-old female driver struck a parked car while she was traveling at a low rate of speed. She is complaining of shortness of breath and chest pain. Your assessment and treatment should include:
options: Breath sounds, history, place in a position of comfort
questions.explanation: All elderly patients should have cardiac monitoring, pulse oximetry, and capnography, if available.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: When transporting a third trimester pregnant trauma patient in spinal motion restriction, you should:
options: Tilt or rotate the board or patient 15-30 degrees to the left
Manually displace the uterus to the right
Secure the patient in the left lateral recumbent position
questions.explanation: Supine hypotension syndrome: acute hypotension in the pregnant patient due to decreased venous return. It usually occurs when the patient is in a supine position with a 20-week or larger uterus (uterus up to umbilicus). The transport of all pregnant trauma patients, if no contraindication exists, should be by one of the following methods to alleviate vena cava compression: Tilt or rotate the backboard 15 to 30 degrees to the patient's left. Elevate the right hip four to six inches (10 to 15 centimeters) with a towel, and manually displace the uterus to the left.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following statements regarding SPO2 is correct?
options: An SPO2 reading of 90% is equivalent to a PAO2 reading of 90 mmHg
As a general rule, any pulse oximeter reading below 95% is cause for concern
You should try to maintain a pulse oximeter reading of 95% or higher in your trauma patient
SPO2 is unreliable in trauma patients and has no value in their assessment
questions.explanation: Try to maintain a pulse oximeter reading of 95% or higher. However, do not withhold oxygen from a patient with a pulse oximeter reading above 95% who also shows signs and symptoms of hypoxia or difficulty breathing.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The purpose of the ITLS Primary Survey is to identify:
questions.explanation: The purpose of the initial assessment is to prioritize the patient and to identify immediately life-threatening conditions.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: You are delivering adequate ventilation volume when:
options: You provide 5-7 ml/kg per of ventilator support
Air begins to leak from the mask seal
You observe bilateral chest rise and fall
questions.explanation: Ventilate with enough volume (8-10 ml per kg body weight) to cause adequate chest rise.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An unresponsive 34-year-old female was struck by a car. You observe asymmetrical chest motion. Following delegation of C-spine control and opening the airway, you should next:
questions.explanation: The signs indicative of chest injury found upon inspection include chest wall contusion, open wounds, subcutaneous emphysema, hemoptysis, distended neck veins, tracheal deviation, asymmetrical chest movement including paradoxical motion, cyanosis, and shock. Initially assisted ventilation with positive pressure.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which one of the following is typically associated with post-traumatic hemorrhage early shock?
options: Loss of 30% to 45% of blood volume
questions.explanation: Early shock is the loss of approximately 15% to 25% of blood volume. That is enough to stimulate slight to moderate tachycardia, pallor, narrowed pulse pressure, thirst, weakness, and possibly delayed capillary refill.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An unresponsive 52-year-old male was struck in the head. Vital signs are BP 134/80, P 88, and R 6 and shallow. You should ventilate to maintain an ETCO2 of:
questions.explanation: Hyperventilation and hypoventilation can cause cerebral ischemia and increased mortality in the TBI patient. Maintaining normal ventilation (not hyperventilation) with high-flow oxygen at a rate of about one breath every 6 to 8 seconds (8 to 10 per minute) to maintain an end-tidal CO2 (ETCO2) of 35-45 mmHg.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 52-year-old male has an isolated knee dislocation. He is alert and oriented, BP 144/84, P 88 and R 20. Pulses distal to the location are absent and you have an extended transport time. You should:
options: Splint the injury in the position found
Gently apply traction in an effort realign the extremity to restore pulses
Place patient in a Trendelenburg position
Flex the knee and splint to the opposite leg
questions.explanation: There are certain exceptions to the general rule of splinting injuries in the position found, including when loss of distal pulse is noted. In that case, especially if you have a long transport time to the appropriate hospital, gentle traction should be applied in an effort to bring the injured extremity into a more anatomic position and restore the distal pulse.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Death in the immediate post-burn period is typically associated with:
questions.explanation: Death in the immediate post-burn period is a consequence of associated trauma or conditions such as airway compromise or smoke inhalation.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is the most reliable early indicator for determining shock in the injured child?
questions.explanation: Early shock (often referred to as compensated shock) is far more difficult to diagnose in a child than in an adult because of a child's ability to hemodynamically compensate and maintain a normal blood pressure despite life-threatening bleeding. Persistent tachycardia is the most reliable early indicator of shock in a child.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Prevention of which of the following would have the greatest impact for reducing trauma in pregnancy?
questions.explanation: Motor vehicle collisions (MVCs) account for 65% to 75% of pregnancy-related trauma in North America.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: When using positive pressure ventilation in a trauma cardiopulmonary arrest patient, you should ventilate:
options: 1 breath every 8 seconds with a tidal volume of 750 cc
12 breaths per minute with a volume of 750 cc
8 breaths per minute with a volume of 500 cc
1 breath every 6 seconds with a volume of 500 cc
questions.explanation: In the TCPA patient, it is reasonable to begin with an assisted ventilation rate of one breath every eight seconds with a tidal volume of 750 cc, which is about eight times per minute using roughly a one-hand squeeze on a standard bag.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The most important factors in determining injuries sustained in a fall are:
options: Distance, impact area on the body, surface struck
Distance, movement during the fall, underlying medical conditions
Distance, underlying medical conditions, surface struck
questions.explanation: The mechanism of injury for falls is vertical deceleration. The types of injuries sustained depend on the following three factors, which you must identify and relay to medical direction: distance of fall; anatomic area impacted; and surface struck.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 34-year-old male is injured in a motor vehicle collision and complains of diffuse abdominal tenderness. The initial assessment reveals fast and shallow respirations, a rapid, weak radial pulse, and pale, cool, diaphoretic skin with no visible external bleeding. You should:
options: Administer oxygen and perform a rapid trauma survey
Immediately evaluate his blood glucose and lactate levels
Complete spinal motion restriction and transport immediately
Obtain SAMPLE history, vital signs and cardiac monitoring
questions.explanation: If you identify a priority (high-risk) patient, you need to find the cause of the abnormal findings to determine if this is a load-and-go patient.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following assessment findings would be an indication for immediate intubation?
questions.explanation: The presence of snoring or stridor or wheezes should alert the rescuer to the presence of airway obstruction. Ultimately, the placement of a 'rescue' ventilation device (BIAD, such as laryngeal mask airway) or endotracheal tube may be required.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An unrestrained 17-year-old female driver rear-ends a cement truck at a high rate of speed. She is unconscious, pale, cool and clammy, has fast, labored respirations and fast, thready radial pulses. Her neck veins are flat, trachea midline, chest motion is asymmetrical, and breath sounds on the left are absent. You should suspect:
questions.explanation: Clinical signs of shock may be apparent. The neck veins are usually flat secondary to profound hypovolemia, but may very rarely be distended due to mediastinal compression. Other signs of hemothorax include decreased breath sounds and dullness to percussion on the affected side.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The lateral approach for chest decompression is preferred:
options: When the access to the anterior approach is limited or unsafe
To minimize liver or spleen laceration
To help stabilize the catheter during movement or transport
questions.explanation: Military and tactical medical personnel prefer the lateral approach because in a tactical situation it has the advantage of allowing decompression while keeping body armor in place.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is not caused by the release of catecholamines during shock?
questions.explanation: Catecholamines (epinephrine and norepinephrine) increase both the rate and strength of the heart's contractions, constrict peripheral arterial blood vessels and affects the sweat glands. Hypotension is the result of the inability to compensate.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What treatment is not indicated in the routine management of the patient with a suspected traumatic brain injury?
options: Maintain a pulse oximetry reading between 94%-99%
Fluid resuscitation to a BP of 110-120 systolic if the patient is hypotensive
Ventilating to obtain an ETCO2 of 35-45 mmHg
Hyperventilating to obtain an ETCO2 of less than 30 mmHg
questions.explanation: Hyperventilation actually has only a slight effect on brain swelling, but causes a significant decrease in cerebral perfusion from that same vasoconstriction, resulting in cerebral hypoxia. Thus, both hyperventilation and hypoventilation can cause cerebral ischemia and increased mortality in the TBI patient. Maintaining normal ventilation (not hyperventilation) with high-flow oxygen at a rate of about one breath every 6 to 8 seconds (8 to 10 per minute) to maintain an end-tidal CO2 (ETCO2) of 35-45 mmHg is very important. Prophylactic hyperventilation for TBI is no longer recommended.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Spinal motion restriction should be considered in all of the following except:
options: Blunt trauma with altered level of consciousness
questions.explanation: Immobilization onto a long backboard is not indicated in penetrating wounds of torso, neck, or head unless there is clinical evidence of a spine injury.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which treatment is indicated for the patient who is entrapped and has an isolated crush injury to the lower extremity?
options: Apply tourniquet proximal to the injury
Apply air splint to compress extremity
Locate artery proximal to the wound and apply pressure
questions.explanation: If administration of fluids or medications prior to releasing the entrapped body area is not possible, consider application of a tourniquet proximal to the injury site on the extremity. Although application of the tourniquet will reduce systemic toxin release, crush syndrome will continue to develop.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following best describes how carbon monoxide causes hypoxia?
options: The carbon monoxide atoms bind with hemoglobin and lock out the oxygen
The carbon monoxide atoms block out oxygen in the bloodstream
The carbon monoxide atoms break up the oxygen
The carbon monoxide atoms turn oxygen into carbon dioxide
questions.explanation: Carbon monoxide binds to hemoglobin (257 times stronger than oxygen), resulting in the hemoglobin being unable to transport oxygen. Patients quickly become hypoxic even in the presence of low concentrations of carbon monoxide.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An unresponsive 5-year-old female was struck by a car. She presents with retractions and nasal flaring. The airway is patent, breathing is fast and shallow, and the carotid pulse is weak and slow. You should:
questions.explanation: Note whether the child is 'working' to breathe, demonstrated by subcostal or suprasternal retractions, nasal flaring, or grunting. If you have any doubt that the child is breathing adequately on his or her own, immediately assist the child's breathing.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following statements concerning treatment of shock in the pregnant burn patient is true?
options: Oxygen should be used sparingly so as to avoid oxygen toxicity to the fetus
Vasopressor agents such as dopamine should be used to improve circulation to the fetus
Volume replacement should be given earlier and in larger amounts to the pregnant burn patient
Volume replacement should be given more slowly so as to avoid fluid overloading the fetus
questions.explanation: The overall mortality and morbidity resulting from thermal injuries to the pregnant patient is not markedly different from the non-pregnant patient. However, it is important to remember that the fluid requirement for the pregnant patient is greater than that of the nonpregnant woman.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 17-year-old male is involved in a minor motor vehicle collision and is refusing treatment. The patient has constricted pupils and law enforcement officers on scene suspect drug usage. You suspect the patient ingested:
questions.explanation: Signs and symptoms of narcotic/opiate use are altered mental status, constricted pupils, bradycardia, hypotension, respiratory distress depression and hypothermia.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 49-year-old male was crossing the street and struck by a truck. First responders are doing CPR. The patient is pulseless, apneic and the monitor shows asystole. Your assessment reveals an open skull fracture and deformed pelvis. Based on the ITLS curriculum, which of the following is the most appropriate act?
options: Immediately intubate the patient to secure the airway
Establish intravenous access and administer a 1 liter bolus
Establish intravenous access and administer 2 mg of atropine
Resuscitative efforts should be discontinued, unless prohibited by law
questions.explanation: Resuscitation should be withheld in cases of: (a) blunt trauma with no breathing, pulse, or organized rhythm on ECG on EMS arrival at the scene; (b) penetrating trauma with no breathing, pulse, pupillary reflexes, spontaneous movement, or organized ECG activity; (c) any trauma with injuries obviously incompatible with life (such as decapitation); (d) any trauma with evidence of significant time lapse since pulselessness, including dependent lividity, rigor mortis, etc.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 23-year-old male fell from the roof of a building and reports that he cannot feel his arms or legs. You should first:
options: Apply a cervical collar and transport
Obtain a blood pressure and SAO2
questions.explanation: Once you begin patient assessment in the ITLS Primary Survey, only four things should cause you to interrupt completion of the assessment. You may interrupt the assessment sequence only if (1) the scene becomes unsafe, (2) you must treat exsanguinating hemorrhage, (3) you must treat an airway obstruction, or (4) you must treat cardiac arrest.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: When evaluating a patient prior to intubation, you can visualize the upper half of the tonsils. What would you rate the Mallampati score?
questions.explanation: The Mallampati score ranges from I to IV and is dependent on what structures can be viewed when the mouth is opened.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: When performing a needle decompression, which of the following is the correct anatomical location?
options: Directly under the bottom of the second rib, midclavicular line
Directly under the bottom of the third rib, midclavicular line
Directly over the top of the fourth rib, midaxillary line
Directly over the top of the third rib, midclavicular line
questions.explanation: Insert the needle into the second intercostal space at a 90-degree angle to the superior border of the third rib to avoid the neurovascular bundle.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following assessment findings is associated with neurogenic shock?
options: Increased pulse, normal skin color and temperature
Decreased pulse, normal skin color and temperature
questions.explanation: The clinical presentation of neurogenic shock differs from hemorrhagic shock in that there is no catecholamine release and thus no pallor (vasoconstriction), tachycardia, or sweating. The patient will have a decreased blood pressure, but the heart rate will be normal or slow, and the skin is usually warm, dry, and pink.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What treatment is not indicated in the routine management of the adult patient with a head injury?
options: Fluid resuscitation to a BP of 110-120 systolic
Hyperventilating to obtain an ETCO2 of less than 30 mmHg
questions.explanation: Hyperventilation actually has only a slight effect on brain swelling, but causes a significant decrease in cerebral perfusion from that same vasoconstriction, resulting in cerebral hypoxia. Thus, both hyperventilation and hypoventilation can cause cerebral ischemia and increased mortality in the TBI patient. Maintaining normal ventilation (not hyperventilation) with high-flow oxygen at a rate of about one breath every 6 to 8 seconds (8 to 10 per minute) to maintain an end-tidal CO2 (ETCO2) of 35-45 mmHg is very important. Prophylactic hyperventilation for TBI is no longer recommended.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following statements regarding spinal motion restriction is true?
options: Elderly patients often require padding under the shoulders due to underlying kyphosis.
Smaller children often require more padding under the head because their heads are disproportionately larger.
Elderly patients whose necks have a natural flexed posture will require posterior padding.
Combative patients requiring spinal motion restriction will need to be restrained tighter to ensure that all movement is eliminated.
questions.explanation: Elderly patients whose necks have a naturally flexed posture will require posterior padding.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is not a recommended way to estimate endotracheal tube size for a pediatric patient?
options: Formula: 4 plus the age in years divided by 4
Formula: weight (kg) divided by 5 or weight (lbs.) divided by 10
questions.explanation: There are several ways to choose the right size for the endotracheal tube. The simplest is to refer to your length-based tape system. Other techniques include choosing a tube that is about the same diameter as the tip of the child's little finger or the equation: 4 plus age in years divided by 4.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is not part of the DOPE mnemonic for evaluating an intubation problem?
options: D - Displacement of the endotracheal tube
questions.explanation: DOPE algorithm: D—displacement of the endotracheal tube, O—obstruction of the endotracheal tube, P—pneumothorax development, or E—equipment problems (ventilator failure, loss of oxygen supply).
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Your first priority in the management of a trauma patient is:
options: Open the airway and assess for breathing
Provide ventilator support for your patient
Begin chest compressions if pulses are absent
questions.explanation: If your patient has major external bleeding, you must immediately direct another team member to control it. As team leader, you may need to initially control the bleeding if no other emergency care provider is immediately available.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which patient would likely be a difficult laryngoscopy and intubation?
options: Patients with a history of asthma
questions.explanation: MMAP is a technique for predicting when a patient will be difficult to intubate. MMAP stands for Mallampati, measurement 3-3-1 (the 1 being the patient should be able to protrude the lower jaw such that the lower teeth are 1 cm beyond the upper teeth) atlanto-occipital extension, and pathology.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 23-year-old female complains of the inability to feel her legs after falling down a stairwell. Her respirations are rapid and shallow; radial pulses are rapid and weak; and her skin is cool, clammy and ashen with no obvious external bleeding. She has flat neck veins and an unstable pelvis. You should suspect:
questions.explanation: Hypovolemic shock victims usually have tachycardia, are pale, and have flat neck veins. So, if you find a trauma victim with a fast heart rate, who is pale, with weak radial pulses and flat neck veins.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An unresponsive 27-year-old male struck his head after falling off a bicycle. Vital signs are BP 128/64, P 88, R 18 and GCS 6 (1, 1 & 4). Appropriate care includes all of the following except:
questions.explanation: Endotracheal intubation is still recommended for adults if the airway cannot be maintained or if you cannot maintain adequate oxygenation with supplemental oxygen. There is no reason to routinely intubate patients who are maintaining their airway and have normal oxygen saturation.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A confused 37-year-old male removed from a house fire complains of a headache and nausea. There is no evidence of inhalation burns or external trauma. You should suspect:
questions.explanation: Headaches and nausea are common symptoms of mild carbon monoxide poisoning, secondary to smoke inhalation.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What is the most commonly injured anatomical area of an infant after a fall from a height?
questions.explanation: Young children who fall from higher than a few feet often land on their heads because the head is disproportionately large and heavy compared to the body of a small child.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Through apneic oxygenation, a high oxygen concentration gradient may be maintained via:
questions.explanation: Instilling oxygen via a nasal cannula (>8 liters per minute) maintains a high oxygen concentration gradient from the nasopharynx down to the alveoli. As oxygen is absorbed in the alveoli, more oxygen moves down this gradient into the alveoli, even if there is no ventilation.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An unconscious 18-year-old male is involved in a motor vehicle collision. You find him unrestrained behind the bent steering wheel. He is unconscious with cool, pale and clammy skin. Vitalis are BP 90/40, P 120 and thready and R 30 and shallow. Your assessment reveals that he is blue around the lips, has distended neck veins and tracheal deviation to the right. He also has an asymmetrical chest with absent breath sounds on the left. You should suspect:
questions.explanation: Clinical signs of a tension pneumothorax include dyspnea, anxiety, tachypnea, distended neck veins, and possibly tracheal deviation away from the affected side. Auscultation will reveal diminished breath sounds on the affected side and will be accompanied by hyperresonance when percussed.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 16-year-old male sustained a gunshot wound to the left chest and complains of shortness of breath. He presents with rapid and shallow respirations, absent radial pulses, and cool, clammy, pale skin. His neck veins are distended and there is an absence of breath sounds over the left side. You should suspect:
questions.explanation: Any traumatic or medical condition that slows or prevents the venous return of blood can cause shock by lowering cardiac output and thus oxygen delivery to the tissues. Likewise, anything that obstructs the flow of blood to or through the heart can cause shock.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A minimum systolic blood pressure of ______ mmHg is recommended for the adult patient with a traumatic brain injury and a Glasgow Coma Scale of 7.
questions.explanation: ITLS advocates maintaining systolic blood pressure of at least 110 mmHg in the TBI patient to ensure adequate perfusion, which is higher than the Brain Trauma Foundation guidelines but consistent with those put forth by Arizona EPIC.
questions.subject: Trauma
